questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the SI unit of angular velocity?
questions.hint: Angular velocity measures how fast an object rotates; its unit involves angle divided by time.
questions.explanation: Angular velocity \( \omega \) is defined as \( \Delta \theta / \Delta t \), with angle in radians and time in seconds, giving rad/s. Option D is the unit of angular acceleration, not angular velocity.

source_trace.content_anchor: Angular velocity has units of radians per second (rad/s)

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular velocity is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Angular velocity \( \omega \) measures how fast an object rotates, calculated as change in angular position per unit time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like how fast a fan blade spins: how many "angle units" it turns each second.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \( \omega = \Delta \theta / \Delta t \), the radian is the natural unit of angle in rotational motion, so \( \omega \) is in rad/s.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In calculus terms, \( \omega = d\theta/dt \). The radian is dimensionless, so the unit is s⁻¹, but it is universally written as rad/s.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition: angular velocity = angular displacement / time.
Angular displacement is measured in radians.
Therefore, angular velocity unit is rad/s.
misconceptions: Confusing angular velocity (rad/s) with tangential velocity (m/s).
Thinking rpm is the SI unit; rpm is a common non-SI unit.

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of change of angular displacement with time; SI unit rad/s.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of angular velocity?
flashcard.back: Radians per second (rad/s). From \( \omega = \Delta \theta / \Delta t \) with \( \theta \) in radians, \( t \) in seconds.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the SI unit of angular velocity?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rate of change of angular displacement; unit rad/s.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce radian as the natural angle unit for rotation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the motion of a rigid object around a fixed axis
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Angular velocity = Δθ/Δt, with Δθ in radians, Δt in seconds.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the SI unit of angular acceleration?
questions.hint: Angular acceleration is the rate of change of angular velocity.
questions.explanation: Angular acceleration \( \alpha = \Delta \omega / \Delta t \). Since \( \omega \) is in rad/s and \( t \) in s, the unit is rad/s². Option C is linear acceleration, not angular.

source_trace.content_anchor: Angular acceleration has units of radians per second squared (rad/s²)

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular acceleration is the rate of change of angular velocity with respect to time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Angular acceleration \( \alpha \) indicates how quickly the angular velocity changes; its SI unit follows from \( \alpha = \Delta \omega / \Delta t \).
explanations_tiered.beginner: It's like how quickly a spinning wheel speeds up or slows down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \( \omega \) is in rad/s and \( t \) is in seconds, the change in \( \omega \) per second gives rad/s².
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \( \alpha = d\omega/dt \), \( \omega \) has unit s⁻¹ (since rad is dimensionless), so \( \alpha \) has unit s⁻², universally written as rad/s².
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: angular acceleration = change in angular velocity / time.
misconceptions: Confusing angular acceleration (rad/s²) with linear acceleration (m/s²).
Thinking rad/s is the unit of angular acceleration.

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of change of angular velocity; SI unit rad/s².

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of angular acceleration?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the SI unit of angular acceleration?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rate of change of angular velocity; unit rad/s².
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the motion of a rigid object around a fixed axis
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. α = Δω/Δt, with Δω in rad/s, Δt in s → rad/s².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.structural_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.question: Which equation correctly gives the final angular velocity \( \omega_f \) for constant angular acceleration \( \alpha \) over time \( t \)?
questions.hint: This is the rotational analog of \( v_f = v_0 + at \).
questions.explanation: For constant angular acceleration, the change in angular velocity is \( \alpha t \), so \( \omega_f = \omega_0 + \alpha t \). Option B is the equation for angular displacement, not final angular velocity.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
source_trace.structural_element: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: The equation \( \omega_f = \omega_0 + \alpha t \) gives the final angular velocity after time t under constant angular acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This rotational kinematics equation is analogous to linear motion's \( v_f = v_0 + at \), where \( \alpha \) replaces \( a \).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel speeds up steadily, its final spin rate is the starting rate plus how much it increased each second times the number of seconds.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrating \( \alpha = d\omega/dt \) with constant \( \alpha \): \( \int_{\omega_0}^{\omega_f} d\omega = \int_0^t \alpha dt \Rightarrow \omega_f - \omega_0 = \alpha t \).
reasoning_steps: Start with definition of constant angular acceleration: \( \alpha = (\omega_f - \omega_0)/t \).
Multiply both sides by t: \( \alpha t = \omega_f - \omega_0 \).

formula_analysis.name: Angular velocity as a function of time (constant α)

misconceptions: Adding the \( \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2 \) term (for displacement) to velocity equation.
Forgetting that \( \alpha t \) is the total change in angular velocity.

glossary_entry.definition: For constant angular acceleration, final angular velocity = initial + α × time.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for final angular velocity under constant angular acceleration?
flashcard.back: \( \omega_f = \omega_0 + \alpha t \), analogous to \( v_f = v_0 + at \).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Write the equation for final angular velocity under constant angular acceleration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: ωf = ω0 + αt for constant α.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out analogy with linear motion to aid memory.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equations of motion with constant angular acceleration
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Rotational analog of vf = v0 + at.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.structural_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.question: For constant angular acceleration \( \alpha \), which equation gives the angular displacement \( \Delta \theta \) after time \( t \)?
questions.hint: This is the rotational analog of \( \Delta x = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2 \).
questions.explanation: The correct kinematic equation for angular displacement under constant \( \alpha \) is \( \Delta \theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \). Option C applies only if \( \omega_0 = 0 \) (starting from rest).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
source_trace.structural_element: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular displacement under constant angular acceleration is given by \( \Delta \theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This equation accounts for displacement due to initial angular velocity plus additional displacement from angular acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel starts spinning and also speeds up, its total angle turned is from the starting speed plus extra from getting faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Derived from \( \omega_{\text{avg}} = (\omega_0 + \omega_f)/2 \) and \( \omega_f = \omega_0 + \alpha t \), then \( \Delta \theta = \omega_{\text{avg}} t \).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrate \( \omega(t) = \omega_0 + \alpha t \) to get \( \theta(t) - \theta_0 = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \).
misconceptions: Using \( \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \) alone and omitting \( \omega_0 t \) when starting from non-zero initial speed.
Confusing angular displacement equation with angular velocity equation.

glossary_entry.definition: Δθ = ω₀t + ½αt² for constant angular acceleration.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for angular displacement under constant angular acceleration?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give the angular displacement formula for constant angular acceleration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Δθ = ω₀t + ½αt² for constant α.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that both terms (initial velocity and acceleration) contribute to displacement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equations of motion with constant angular acceleration
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Rotational analog of Δx = v₀t + ½at².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
questions.structural_anchor: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
questions.question: A wheel of radius \( r = 0.5 \, \text{m} \) rotates with angular speed \( \omega = 10 \, \text{rad/s} \). What is the linear (tangential) speed \( v \) of a point on the rim?
questions.explanation: Tangential speed \( v = r \omega = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \, \text{m/s} \). Option A incorrectly divides \( r / \omega \), while option D multiplies incorrectly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
source_trace.structural_element: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities

explanations_tiered.definition: Tangential speed is the linear speed of a point on a rotating object, given by \( v = r \omega \).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All points on a rigid rotating body have the same angular speed \( \omega \), but tangential speed increases with radius according to \( v = r \omega \).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A point farther from the center moves faster in meters per second than a point near the center, even though both turn at the same rate.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From arc length \( s = r \theta \), divide by time: \( v = r \omega \). This relates linear and angular speeds.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Differentiating \( s = r \theta \) (with constant \( r \)) gives \( ds/dt = r \, d\theta/dt \), hence \( v = r \omega \).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall the relationship \( v = r \omega \).
reasoning_steps: Identify the formula connecting linear and angular speed: \( v = r \omega \).
Plug in the given radius and angular speed.
Compute the product to find tangential speed.

formula_analysis.name: Tangential speed
formula_analysis.derivation: From \( s = r\theta \), differentiate: \( v = r \omega \).

misconceptions: Using \( v = \omega / r \) (inverting the relationship).
Thinking all points on a wheel have the same linear speed (false; only same angular speed).

glossary_entry.definition: Linear speed of a point on a rotating object; equals radius times angular speed.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate tangential speed from angular speed and radius?
flashcard.back: \( v = r \omega \). Derived from \( s = r\theta \) divided by time.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A 0.5 m radius wheel spins at 10 rad/s. Find rim speed.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Linear speed of a point on a rotating object, v = rω.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that all points share ω, but v varies with r.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to rotational motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
questions.structural_anchor: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
questions.question: A wheel has angular acceleration \( \alpha = 2.0 \, \text{rad/s}^2 \) and radius \( r = 0.8 \, \text{m} \). What is the tangential acceleration \( a_t \) of a point on the rim?
questions.explanation: Tangential acceleration \( a_t = r \alpha = 0.8 \times 2.0 = 1.6 \, \text{m/s}^2 \). Option A incorrectly divides \( r / \alpha \), and option C uses \( \alpha \) alone without multiplying by radius.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities
source_trace.structural_element: Relationship between angular motion and translational motion quantities

explanations_tiered.definition: Tangential acceleration is the linear acceleration of a point on a rotating object, given by \( a_t = r \alpha \).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a rigid body, tangential acceleration at a point is radius times angular acceleration, analogous to \( a = r \alpha \).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel spins faster and faster, a point on the edge speeds up more quickly in meters per second each second than a point near the center.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \( v = r \omega \), differentiate with respect to time (r constant): \( dv/dt = r \, d\omega/dt \), so \( a_t = r \alpha \).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Taking time derivative of \( \vec{v} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} \) gives tangential component \( a_t = r \alpha \) when \( \alpha \) is perpendicular to \( r \).
reasoning_steps: Plug in radius and angular acceleration.

formula_analysis.name: Tangential acceleration
formula_analysis.derivation: Differentiate \( v = r\omega \) with respect to time.

misconceptions: Using \( a_t = \alpha / r \) (inverting).
Confusing tangential acceleration with centripetal acceleration (\( a_c = v^2/r \) or \( r\omega^2 \)).

glossary_entry.definition: Linear acceleration of a point on a rotating object tangent to its circular path.

flashcard.front: How do you find tangential acceleration from angular acceleration?
flashcard.back: \( a_t = r \alpha \), where r is the radius from the axis of rotation.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A wheel (r=0.8 m) has α=2.0 rad/s². Find tangential acceleration.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish tangential (changes speed) from centripetal (changes direction).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to rotational motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Torque is defined as the rotational effect of a force. Which equation correctly gives the magnitude of torque?
questions.hint: Only the perpendicular component of force produces torque.
questions.explanation: Torque magnitude \( \tau = rF \sin \theta \), where \( \theta \) is the angle between \( \vec{r} \) (lever arm) and \( \vec{F} \). Option B uses cosine, which gives the component parallel to \( r \), not perpendicular.

explanations_tiered.definition: Torque is the rotational equivalent of force, causing angular acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Torque magnitude = lever arm distance × force × sine of the angle between them; only the perpendicular force component contributes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pushing a door near the hinge (small r) or at an angle makes it harder to open; torque depends on both how hard and where you push.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Only the component of force perpendicular to the radius vector produces rotation: \( \tau = r (F \sin \theta) = rF \sin \theta \).
reasoning_steps: Find angle θ between r and F.
misconceptions: Using cosine instead of sine (cosine gives component parallel to r, which does not produce torque).
Forgetting that r is perpendicular distance from pivot to line of action (lever arm).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: τ = rF sin θ. Perpendicular component matters.

glossary_entry.definition: Rotational effect of a force; magnitude τ = rF sin θ.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for torque magnitude?
flashcard.back: \( \tau = rF \sin \theta \), where θ is the angle between r and F.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate torque magnitude?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: τ = rF sin θ, where θ is the angle between position vector r and force F.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rotational effect of force; τ = rF sin θ.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that torque is a vector, direction given by right-hand rule.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define torque in terms of force and distance from axis of rotation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Torque = rF sin θ, from τ = r × F.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A force of \( F = 20 \, \text{N} \) is applied perpendicularly at the end of a wrench handle of length \( r = 0.30 \, \text{m} \). What torque is applied?
questions.explanation: With \( \theta = 90^\circ \), \( \sin 90^\circ = 1 \), so \( \tau = rF = 0.30 \times 20 = 6.0 \, \text{N·m} \). Option A incorrectly divides r/F, and option D multiplies incorrectly with wrong units.

calculation_steps: Torque for perpendicular force: τ = rF

explanations_tiered.definition: Torque is the product of lever arm length and perpendicular force component.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When force is perpendicular to the lever arm, torque simplifies to τ = rF.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pushing at a right angle on a wrench gives the most turning effect for a given force.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since θ = 90°, sin 90° = 1, τ = rF sin θ = rF.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From τ = rF sin θ, when sin θ = 1 (force perpendicular), torque is maximized for given r and F.
worked_solution: Step 1: Force is perpendicular → θ = 90°, sin θ = 1.
reasoning_steps: Note that force is perpendicular to wrench, so sin 90° = 1.

formula_analysis.name: Torque (perpendicular force)
formula_analysis.derivation: Special case of τ = rF sin θ with θ = 90°.

misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply by the lever arm (using just force).
Using τ = F/r instead of rF.

glossary_entry.definition: When force is perpendicular to lever arm, torque = rF.

flashcard.front: A 20 N perpendicular force on a 0.30 m wrench produces what torque?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find torque: 20 N perpendicular force, 0.30 m wrench.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rotational effect; τ = rF when force perpendicular.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that torque depends on perpendicular distance from pivot.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Express torque in terms of moment of inertia and angular acceleration

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Moment of inertia is the rotational analog of what linear quantity?
questions.hint: Just as mass resists linear acceleration, moment of inertia resists angular acceleration.
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia \( I \) measures an object's resistance to changes in rotational motion, analogous to mass in linear motion. In \( \tau = I\alpha \), \( I \) corresponds to \( m \) in \( F = ma \).

source_trace.content_anchor: moment of inertia is the quantitative measure of rotational inertia, just as mass is the quantitative measure of linear inertia

explanations_tiered.definition: Moment of inertia is the measure of an object's resistance to angular acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Moment of inertia \( I \) depends on mass distribution about the axis; it plays the same role in rotation that mass plays in translation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A heavy merry-go-round is hard to spin (large I). Light or compact objects spin easily (small I).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In \( \tau = I\alpha \), \( I \) is analogous to \( m \) in \( F = ma \). Larger \( I \) means larger torque needed for same \( \alpha \).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a point mass, \( I = mr^2 \); for extended bodies, \( I = \sum m_i r_i^2 \). Units: kg·m².
reasoning_steps: Recall Newton's second law for rotation: τ = Iα.
Identical form shows I corresponds to m.
misconceptions: Confusing moment of inertia with torque or angular momentum.
Thinking moment of inertia is the same for all axes (it depends on axis choice).

glossary_entry.definition: Measure of rotational inertia; resistance to angular acceleration.

flashcard.front: What linear quantity is moment of inertia analogous to?
flashcard.back: Mass. \( \tau = I\alpha \) (rotational) is analogous to \( F = ma \) (linear).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the rotational analog of mass?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Moment of inertia (I). In τ = Iα, I corresponds to m in F = ma.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rotational analog of mass; resistance to angular acceleration.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use τ = Iα vs F = ma to reinforce analogy.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define moment of inertia in terms of point mass and distance from axis
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. I is rotational inertia, analogous to mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two point masses, \( m_1 = 2.0 \, \text{kg} \) at \( r_1 = 0.5 \, \text{m} \) and \( m_2 = 1.0 \, \text{kg} \) at \( r_2 = 1.0 \, \text{m} \), are attached to a massless rod rotating about its center. What is the moment of inertia?
questions.hint: For point masses, \( I = \sum m_i r_i^2 \).
questions.explanation: \( I = m_1 r_1^2 + m_2 r_2^2 = (2.0 \times 0.5^2) + (1.0 \times 1.0^2) = (2.0 \times 0.25) + 1.0 = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5 \, \text{kg·m}^2 \). Option A omits the second mass, option B underestimates.

explanations_tiered.definition: Moment of inertia for a system of point masses is the sum of each mass times the square of its distance from the axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each mass contributes \( m_i r_i^2 \) to the total moment of inertia; farther masses contribute more because \( r^2 \) grows rapidly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A mass far from the axis is harder to rotate than an equal mass near the axis — its contribution to I is larger.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For continuous bodies, \( I = \int r^2 \, dm \). For discrete masses, \( I = \sum m_i r_i^2 \).
reasoning_steps: Write the formula for moment of inertia of point masses: I = Σ m_i r_i².

formula_analysis.name: Moment of inertia for point masses

misconceptions: Using r instead of r² (linear, not squared).
Forgetting to sum over all masses.

glossary_entry.definition: Sum of mᵢrᵢ² for all point masses in a system.

flashcard.front: A 2 kg mass at 0.5 m and 1 kg mass at 1.0 m rotate about center. Find I.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Two masses: 2 kg at 0.5 m, 1 kg at 1.0 m from axis. Find I.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: I = Σ mᵢrᵢ² for point masses.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that r is squared, so distance greatly affects I.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define moment of inertia in terms of point mass and distance from axis
